questions.questionText: With reference to Madanapalle of Andhra Pradesh, which one of the following statements is correct?

options.a: Pingali Venkayya designed the tricolour Indian National Flag here.
options.b: Pattabhi Sitaramaiah led the Quit India Movement of Andhra region from here.
options.c: Rabindranath Tagore translated the National Anthem from Bengali to English here.
options.d: Madame Blavatsky and Colonel Olcott set up headquarters of Theosophical Society first her

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.**The National anthem was first sung outside Calcutta by Rabindranath Tagore**himself during a session at the**Besant **Theosophical College in Madanapalle, Andhra Pradesh, on 28th Feb 1919**, when Tagore went to college and sang the song. Then deputy director of the university (also an expert in European music and wife of the Irish poet James Cousins), asked Tagore to create an English translation of the song and establish the musical notation. Tagore then translated the Bengali song to English and the tune was composed by Margaret with the girls in the college singing the chorus.

statementLines: With reference to the Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act of 1919, consider the following statements:
I. It was enacted on the recommendation of the Strachey Commission.
II. It allowed the detention of political prisoners without trial or judicial review for two years.
III. It was repealed as a consequence of the Gandhi-Irwin Pact of 1931.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act of 1919, consider the following statements: I. It was enacted on the recommendation of the Strachey Commission. II. It allowed the detention of political prisoners without trial or judicial review for two years. III. It was repealed as a consequence of the Gandhi-Irwin Pact of 1931. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **

Statement I is incorrect. **The Rowlatt Act was officially called the Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act 1919. It was**based on the recommendations of the Rowlatt Commission (Sedition Committee), headed by the British judge, **Sir Sidney Rowlatt. **The Richard Strachey Commission was created to develop a general strategy and principles to deal with the famines in India. It was the first Famine commission established to look into causes of the**1876-78**famine chaired by Sir Richard Strachey. It led to development of Famine Code in 1883 thus establishing principles of famine prevention and relief.**

Statement II is correct. **The Act essentially sought to criminalize dissent, empowered the government to suppress political activities, and allowed the detention of political prisoners without trial or judicial review for two years. Thus, it allowed the detention of political prisoners without trial or judicial review for two years.**Mahatma Gandhi referred to it as a “Black Law”. 

Statement III is incorrect. **In 1919, Gandhiji launched a nationwide protest against the Rowlatt Act. The protests spread throughout India, and the British authorities were eventually forced to repeal the Rowlatt Act in 1922.

statementLines: In the context of Jallianwala Bagh Massacre during the freedom struggle of India, consider the following
statements:
I. Mahatma Gandhi gave up the title of Kaiser-i-Hind, bestowed by the British for his work during the Boer War in protest against the massacre.
II. The Hunter Committee, which was set up to investigate the Massacre, did not have any Indian as members.
III. Sardar Udham Singh assassinated Brigadier General Reginald Dyer who ordered the opening of fire on people in
Jallianwala Bagh.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: In the context of Jallianwala Bagh Massacre during the freedom struggle of India, consider the following statements: I. Mahatma Gandhi gave up the title of Kaiser-i-Hind, bestowed by the British for his work during the Boer War in protest against the massacre. II. The Hunter Committee, which was set up to investigate the Massacre, did not have any Indian as members. III. Sardar Udham Singh assassinated Brigadier General Reginald Dyer who ordered the opening of fire on people in Jallianwala Bagh. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The Jallianwala Bagh massacre (also known as the Amritsar massacre) took place on April 13, 1919 in Amritsar, Punjab. In this massacre, the British troops under the command of General Reginald Dyer opened fire on a large gathering of Indians who had assembled to peacefully protest against the Rowlatt Act. Thus resulted in a huge massacre and over 1000 people were killed. 

Statement I is correct. **The Bengali poet and Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore renounced the knighthood that he had received in 1915 to protest against the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre.**Mahatma Gandhi gave up the title of Kaiser-i-Hind in protest of the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre. **The title was bestowed by the British for his work during the Boer War.**

Statement II is incorrect.**On October 14, 1919, the Disorders Inquiry Committee was formed to inquire about the Jallianwala Bagh massacre. It later came to be known as the Hunter Commission.**There were three Indian members in the Hunter committee n**amely Sir Chimanlal Harilal Setalvad, Pandit Jagat Narayan and Sardar Sahibzada Sultan Ahmad Khan. In the final report submitted in March 1920, the Committee unanimously condemned Dyer’s actions. However, the Hunter Committee did not impose any penal or disciplinary action against General Dyer. However the report justified the declaration of martial law and its implementation as not oppressive. The Indian National Congress rejected the findings of the Hunter commission. The Indian National Congress objected to the Committee’s justification of declaration of martial law and Dyer’s firing at the mob.**

Statement III is incorrect.Brigadier-General Reginald Edward Harry Dyer ordered troops**to surround the Bagh and launched indiscriminate firing on the assembled men, women and children.**Lieutenant Governor of Punjab Michael O’ Dwyer imposed martial rule **in Lahore and Amritsar on April 11. He sent Reginald Dyer from the Jalandhar cantonment to Amritsar to impose martial law and restore order.**Michael O’ Dwyer, the officer to order martial law, was assassinated in 1940**by Sardar Udham Singh,** Sardar Udham Singh was present at Jallianwala Bagh and escaped the atrocities.

statementLines: With reference to the movements/protests associated with Mahatma Gandhi in India, consider the following information:
Movement/Protest Primary Objective of the Movement/Protest Leader Associated
I. Champaran Satyagraha Abolition of Tinkathia system of indigo cultivation.
Rajendra Prasad
II. Kheda Satyagraha Remission of taxes following the failure of the agricultural harvest that year.
Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel
III. Ahmedabad Mill Strike Reduction in export duties imposed on Indian textiles Ambalal Sarabhai
In
how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to the movements/protests associated with Mahatma Gandhi in India, consider the following information: Movement/Protest Primary Objective of the Movement/Protest Leader Associated I. Champaran Satyagraha Abolition of Tinkathia system of indigo cultivation. Rajendra Prasad II. Kheda Satyagraha Remission of taxes following the failure of the agricultural harvest that year. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel III. Ahmedabad Mill Strike Reduction in export duties imposed on Indian textiles Ambalal Sarabhai In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **After his arrival in India, Gandhi was involved in three struggles—in Champaran, Ahmedabad and Kheda.**Information in Row I is correct. **Champaran Satyagraha of 1917 was the first civil disobedience movement of Gandhi in India. The Champaran Satyagraha was launched in the Champaran district of northern Bihar. It was**launched against the **Tinkathia system of indigo cultivation in Champaran.**The cultivators of Champaran were forced into growing indigo by British planters/estate owners. The Champaran tenants were forced to plant three out of every twenty parts of his land with indigo for his landlord. This system was called Tinkathia. During the Satyagrahe, he was**joined by Rajendra Prasad, Mazhar-ul-Haq, Mahadeo Desai, Narhari Parekh, and J.B. **Kripalani. **Gandhi launched the Champaran Satyagraha** to abolish the Tinkathia system, obtain security of tenure for **the peasants as well as the freedom to cultivate the crops of their choice.**Other popular leaders associated with Champaran Satyagraha were Brajkishore Prasad, Anugrah Narayan Sinha, Ramnavmi Prasad and Shambhusharan Varma.**Information in Row II is correct. **The Kheda Satyagraha of 1918 was organised to** protest against the oppressive tax **imposed on peasants despite them having suffered huge losses due to crop failure in **1917-18** due to droughts. **The distressed farmers of the Kheda district (Gujarat)**demanded remission of revenue **for the year 1919 to reduce their sufferings, but the colonial government paid no heed to their concerns.Also government declared their lands will be seized if taxes are not paid on time. As part of the experiment, Mahatma Gandhi advised the peasants to withhold payment of revenue till their demand for its remission was met.**Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel and a group of other devoted Gandhians, namely, Narahari Parikh, Mohanlal Pandya and **Ravi Shankar Vyas **played an important role in organising the villagers and providing local political leadership. Ultimately, the government agreed to suspend the tax for the year in question, and for the next year,reduce the increase in rate and return all the confiscated property.**Information in Row III is incorrect. **Ahmedabad Mill Strike of 1918 was**a hunger strike initiated by Gandhi in **support of the Ahmedabad mill workers over the issue of discontinuation of the plague bonus. **The mill workers were getting plague bonuses on top of their basic wages due to the ongoing epidemic. The workers agitated when the bonus was stopped by mill owners. The workers were also demanding a rise of 50 per cent in their wages so that they could manage in the times of wartime inflation while mill owners were ready to give only a 20% hike. The workers of the mill turned to**Anusuya Sarabhai **for help in fighting for justice. Anusuya Behn went to Gandhi and asked him to intervene.
Gandhi advised the workers to go on strike and demand a 35 percent increase in wages instead of 50%. Ultimately, it was agreed to give the workers a 35 percent increase in wages.
Ambalal Sarabhai was one of the mill owners as well as the President of Ahmedabad Mill Owners Association.

statementLines: Which among the following statements about Mahatma Gandhi’s Satyagraha is/are correct?
1. It was during the course of his campaign against racialism in South Africa that Gandhiji first applied Satyagraha.
2. The two vital ingredients of Satyagraha are ‘truth’ and ‘non-violence’.
3. The Satyagrahi resists evil by inflicting suffering on himself and not by inflicting suffering on the opponent.
4. In India, Satyagraha was first tried by Gandhiji in Champaran.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which among the following statements about Mahatma Gandhi’s Satyagraha is/are correct? 1. It was during the course of his campaign against racialism in South Africa that Gandhiji first applied Satyagraha. 2. The two vital ingredients of Satyagraha are ‘truth’ and ‘non-violence’. 3. The Satyagrahi resists evil by inflicting suffering on himself and not by inflicting suffering on the opponent. 4. In India, Satyagraha was first tried by Gandhiji in Champaran. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Statement 1 is correct: **Mahatma Gandhi**developed and employed the concept of Satyagraha during his years in **South Africa **while leading the Indian community in their struggle against racial discrimination and injustice.**

Statement 2 is correct**: Gandhiji emphasized that Satyagraha is based on the principles of**truth (Satya) and **nonviolence (Ahimsa). **These two principles were considered fundamental to the philosophy and practice of Satyagraha.**

Statement 3 is correct: **In Satyagraha, the**emphasis is on self-suffering and self-restraint rather than causing harm **or suffering to the opponent**. The Satyagrahi willingly undergoes suffering, such as imprisonment, fasting, or other forms of self-denial, to demonstrate their commitment to truth and non-violence.**

Statement 4 is correct: **One of the**early instances of Gandhiji’s application of Satyagraha in India was during the **Champaran Satyagraha in 1917**. It was a movement against the oppressive indigo plantation system in Bihar’s Champaran district.

statementLines: With reference to Swaraj Party in the Indian Freedom struggle, consider the following statements:
I.
C. R. Das and Motilal Nehru were leaders associated with the Party.
II. The members of the party advocated for participating in the elections to the legislative councils to expose the hollowness of British rule in India from within these councils.
III. It introduced the Public Safety Bill (1928) in the Central Legislative Assembly to safeguard civil liberties.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Swaraj Party in the Indian Freedom struggle, consider the following statements: I. C. R. Das and Motilal Nehru were leaders associated with the Party. II. The members of the party advocated for participating in the elections to the legislative councils to expose the hollowness of British rule in India from within these councils. III. It introduced the Public Safety Bill (1928) in the Central Legislative Assembly to safeguard civil liberties. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Swaraj Party, also known as Congress-Khilafat Swarajya Party or Swarajist Party, was formed over the question of entry into provincial councils following the withdrawal of the Non-cooperation movement in 1922.**

Statement I is correct: C. R. Das and Motilal Nehru formed the Swaraj Party**within the Congress to argue for a return to council politics. They felt that it was important to oppose British policies within the councils, argue for reform and also demonstrate that these councils were not truly democratic.**

Statement II is correct: **The members of Swaraj Party**advocated for participating in the elections to the Legislative **Councils to expose the hollowness of British rule from within. **They wanted an end to the boycott of legislative councils so that the nationalists could enter them to expose the basic weaknesses of these assemblies and use these councils as an arena of political struggle to arouse popular enthusiasm. They**wanted to ‘end or mend’ these councils, **i.e., if the government did not respond to the nationalists’ demands, then they would obstruct the working of these councils.**

Statement III is incorrect: **One of their noteworthy**achievements of the Swarajya Party was the defeat of the Public **Safety Bill in 1928, **which was aimed at empowering the Government to deport undesirable and subversive foreigners mainly the socialists and communists. The Government was alarmed by the spread of socialist and communist ideas and believed that a crucial role was being played by the British and other foreign activists being sent by the Comintern, an international organisation that advocated communism, headed by the Soviet Union. Knowledge Base:**No-Changers:**- It was the other school of thought led by**C. Rajagopalachari, Vallabhbhai Patel, Rajendra Prasad and M.A. **Ansari. **- The ‘No-changers’**opposed council entry, advocated concentration on constructive work,** and continuation of boycott and non-cooperation, and quiet preparation for resumption of the suspended civil disobedience programme.

statementLines: Consider the following events:
I. Appointment of Nehru Committee to draft a Constitution for India.
II. Establishment of the Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (FICCI).
III. Formation of Hindustan Republic Association.
Which of the following is the correct chronological sequence of the above events?
questions.questionText: Consider the following events: I. Appointment of Nehru Committee to draft a Constitution for India. II. Establishment of the Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (FICCI). III. Formation of Hindustan Republic Association. Which of the following is the correct chronological sequence of the above events?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**The correct chronological sequence of above events is: Formation of Hindustan Republic Association (1924) - Establishment of Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (1927) - Appointment of Nehru Committee to draft a Constitution for India (1928) -**Formation of Hindustan Republic Association (1924):**The**Hindustan Republican Association (HRA)**was founded in**October 1924 at Kanpur**. It emerged as a revolutionary organisation aiming to overthrow colonial rule through**armed struggle**and to establish a**federal republic based on adult franchise**. -**Establishment of Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (1927): **During the First World War (**1914-19**18),** Indian merchants and industrialists **had made huge profits and became powerful. Keen on expanding their business, they now reacted against colonial policies that restricted business activities. They wanted protection against imports of foreign goods, and a rupee-sterling foreign exchange ratio that would discourage imports.**To organise business interests,**they**formed**the Indian Industrial and Commercial Congress in 1920 and**the Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (FICCI) in 1927. **-**Appointment of Nehru Committee to draft a Constitution for India (1928): **The Nehru Committee was**appointed **at an All Parties Conference met in February 1928 **to draft a Constitution for India. The committee included Tej Bahadur Sapru, Subhash Bose, M.S. Aney, Mangal Singh, Ali Imam, Shuab Qureshi and G.R. Pradhan as its members. The report was finalised by August 1928. The**recommendations**of the Nehru Committee included**dominion status for India **on lines of self-governing dominions,**rejection of separate electorates, formation of **linguistic provinces **among others.

statementLines: Before launching the Civil Disobedience movement in India, Mahatma Gandhi served a memorandum with certain demands of the people to the Viceroy Lord Irwin. In this context consider the following demands:
I. Acceptance of Purna Swaraj as the constitutional status of India
II. Removal of land revenue as a subject from the legislative control
III. Abolition of the salt tax
IV. Introduction of provincial autonomy under the federal structure
V. Abolition of the Criminal Investigation Department (CID)
How many of the above were part of the memorandum served by Gandhiji to the Viceroy?
questions.questionText: Before launching the Civil Disobedience movement in India, Mahatma Gandhi served a memorandum with certain demands of the people to the Viceroy Lord Irwin. In this context consider the following demands: I. Acceptance of Purna Swaraj as the constitutional status of India II. Removal of land revenue as a subject from the legislative control III. Abolition of the salt tax IV. Introduction of provincial autonomy under the federal structure V. Abolition of the Criminal Investigation Department (CID) How many of the above were part of the memorandum served by Gandhiji to the Viceroy?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**Mahatma Gandhi presented his eleven demands relating to administrative reform to the Viceroy Lord Irwin on January 31, 1930, before launching the Civil Disobedience Movement. This memorandum submitted by Mahatma Gandhi to Lord Irwin contained 11 points, which were as follows: 1. The Rupee-Sterling ratio should be reduced to 1s 4d per rupee.**2. Land revenue should be**reduced by half (50%) and**made a subject of legislative control (and not taken out of their **control). Thus Option II is incorrect**3. Salt tax should be abolished and also the government monopoly over manufacturing of salt. Thus Option III is **correct**4. Reduction of military expenditure by fifty percent to begin with. 5. Reduction of salaries of the highest-grade services by half. 6. Protection for Indian textile and coastal shipping(Passage of the Coastal Traffic Reservation Bill.) 7. Acceptance of postal reservation bill. 8. Discharge of all political prisoners except those condemned for murder. 9. Allowing the popular control of Issue of firearm licenses, for self-defence.**10. Abolition of the Criminal Investigation Department (C.I.D.). Thus, Option V is correct**11. Introduce total prohibition With no positive response from the government, Mahatma Gandhi decided to launch the Civil Disobedience movement making Salt the central issue and symbol of the movement.** Option I is incorrect:**Indian National Congress passed at its session at Lahore, 1929 that now onwards its main objective would be to seek Purna Swaraj, complete independence. But the memorandum served by Gandhiji**did not even remotely mention the resolve to attain Purna Swaraj.** Option IV is incorrect: Introduction of provincial autonomy under the federal structure was not one of the eleven **demands** made by Mahatma Gandhi to the British Government on January 31, 1930.

statementLines: In the context of the Lahore session of the Indian National Congress, 1929 consider the following statements:
I. The resolution for Poorn Swaraj (Complete Independence) of India was unanimously adopted at this session.
II. A resolution in this session was unanimously adopted rejecting the two-nation theory.

questions.questionText: In the context of the Lahore session of the Indian National Congress, 1929 consider the following statements: I. The resolution for Poorn Swaraj (Complete Independence) of India was unanimously adopted at this session. II. A resolution in this session was unanimously adopted rejecting the two-nation theory. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **At the end of December 1929, the Indian National Congress (INC) held its annual session in Lahore. Jawaharlal Nehru was elected as the President of the Session.**

Statement I is correct:**Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru played a pivotal role by drafting a resolution, which was**unanimously **passed by the assembly, focusing on the objective of attaining complete independence, known as Purna Swaraj. **This resolution held significant importance in India's freedom movement, serving as a declaration rather than a legally structured document.**

Statement II is incorrect: The Muslim League, led by Muhammad Ali Jinnah, passed the Lahore Resolution, 1940 **calling explicitly for a “separate Muslim State” to be created from British India. **Thus the formal beginning of the Two Nations Theory came up in March 1940. While the INC Session of Lahore happened in**1929-30**, thus it could not have passed a resolution rejecting the two nation theory.

statementLines: With reference to History of Modern India, the book ‘Folklore of Southern India’ was written by:
questions.questionText: With reference to History of Modern India, the book ‘Folklore of Southern India’ was written by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **The ideas of nationalism during the colonial period developed through a movement to revive Indian folklore.**

Option d is correct: **In late-nineteenth-century India, nationalists began recording folk tales sung by bards and they toured villages to gather folk songs and legends. It was essential to preserve this folk tradition in order to discover one’s national identity and restore a sense of pride in one’s past. In Madras,**Natesa Sastri published a massive four
- **volume collection of Tamil folk tales, The Folklore of Southern India. **He believed that folklore was national literature and it was ‘the most trustworthy manifestation of people’s real thoughts and characteristics’.**In Bengal, **Rabindranath Tagore himself began collecting ballads, **nursery rhymes and myths, and led the movement for folk revival.

statementLines: Mahatma Gandhi said that some of his deepest convictions were reflected in a book titled, “Unto this Last’’ and the book transformed his life. What was the message from the book that transformed Mahatma Gandhi?
questions.questionText: Mahatma Gandhi said that some of his deepest convictions were reflected in a book titled, “Unto this Last’’ and the book transformed his life. What was the message from the book that transformed Mahatma Gandhi?

options.a: Uplifting the oppressed and poor is the moral responsibility of an educated man
options.b: The good of individual is contained in the good of all
options.c: The life of celibacy and spiritual pursuit are essential for a noble life
options.d: All the statements (a), (b) and (c) are correct in this context

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**Gandhi translated Unto This Last into Gujarati in 1908 under the title of Sarvodaya (Well Being of All). He summarised Unto This Last’s teachings in these three points:**I. That the good of the individual is contained in the good of all.** II. That a lawyer’s work has the same value as the barber’s, in as much as all have the same right of earning their livelihood from their work.
III. That a life of labour, i.e., the life of the tiller of the soil and the handicraftsman, is the life worth living.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: What was the primary objective behind the establishment of Praja Mandals during the Indian freedom movement? states.
questions.questionText: What was the primary objective behind the establishment of Praja Mandals during the Indian freedom movement? states.

options.a: To function as parallel governments in the rural areas of British Indian provinces.
options.b: To spread revolutionary nationalism through secret societies in both British Indian provinces as well as princely
options.c: To organize armed peasant uprisings against oppressive land revenue systems in the British Indian provinces.
options.d: To promote the development of democratic and national consciousness in princely states.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Praja Mandals (also called Lok Parishads or People's Organizations/Associations) were political bodies formed by **the people of princely states during the Indian freedom struggle**, mainly from the 1910s–1920s onward.**Praja Mithra **Mandali (1917) of Mysore**was perhaps the first such early organisation. - Princely states were not directly under British rule but under Indian rulers (princes/maharajas) who enjoyed internal autonomy while being allied with the British. - The primary objective behind establishing these Praja Mandals was to fight against the autocratic and feudal rule of the princes and demand democratic reforms within the princely states. Thus,**Praja Mandals were organised in a large number of princely states with the development of democratic and **national consciousness. **Their core demands included: **-**Establishment of responsible government **(where rulers would be accountable to elected representatives or legislative bodies, rather than ruling absolutely). -**Protection and extension of civil liberties **(such as freedom of speech, press, association, assembly, and protection from arbitrary arrest or oppression). -**End to oppressive practices **like forced labor (begar), illegal taxes, excessive land revenue burdens, and other feudal exploitations. -**Widening of franchises for the representative assemblies**; and selection of elected members of the legislative councils as ministers. These movements were inspired by and gradually aligned with the broader Indian National Congress-led freedom struggle. Knowledge Base:**All India States Peoples’ Conference (AISPC):**- The**AISPC**was formed in**1927**as an**umbrella organisation of Praja Mandals**functioning across the princely states. - Its core aim was to**coordinate popular political movements**demanding**civil liberties and responsible **government in the princely states.**- The**first session**of the AISPC was held at**Bombay in 1927**, bringing together representatives from several princely states. - The**1939 session of the AISPC at Ludhiana (Punjab)**marked a turning point, as the**Indian National Congress **formally extended support**to the AISPC amid rising political mobilisation in the princely states.**Jawaharlal Nehru**presided over this session**and was elected president, a position he held until**1946**.

Post Independence, she played an important role in the establishment of national bodies such as the All-India Handicrafts Board and National School of Drama. Which of the following Indian women leaders is best described above?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay (1903–1988)**was one of the most multifaceted personalities of the Indian national movement, combining**political activism, social reform, and cultural institution-building. **- She created history by**becoming the first Indian woman to contest a legislative election in 1926, **when she stood for the**Madras Legislative Council. **- During the freedom struggle, she was an**active participant in the Salt Satyagraha (1930)**and was among the**earliest women to be arrested**in the Civil Disobedience Movement. - She was also associated with organizations such as the**All-India Women’s Conference**and**worked **consistently for women’s rights and social reform. **- After independence, Kamaladevi emerged as a**key architect of India’s cultural and handicrafts policy**. She played a central role in reviving**indigenous handicrafts and cottage industries.**- She was closely associated with the establishment of institutions such as the**All-India Handicrafts Board**,**Central Cottage Industries Emporium**,**National School of Drama and Sangeet Natak Academy.**- For her contributions, she was awarded the**Padma Bhushan (1955), Ramon Magasaysay Award (1966) **and later the**Padma Vibhushan (1987). **

statementLines: With reference to the Second Round Table Conference (1931) held in London during the freedom struggle of
India, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. It was held during the tenure of Lord Willingdon as the Viceroy of India.
II. Mahatma Gandhi participated in the Conference as the sole representative of the Indian National Congress.
III. The success of the Second Round Table Conference in attaining its desired objectives resulted in the suspension of the Civil Disobedience Movement in India.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to the Second Round Table Conference (1931) held in London during the freedom struggle of India, which of the following statements is/are correct? I. It was held during the tenure of Lord Willingdon as the Viceroy of India. II. Mahatma Gandhi participated in the Conference as the sole representative of the Indian National Congress. III. The success of the Second Round Table Conference in attaining its desired objectives resulted in the suspension of the Civil Disobedience Movement in India. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The**Second Round Table Conference**was**held in London in 1931**as part of the British Government’s efforts to evolve a constitutional framework for India through consultation with Indian political stakeholders.**

Statement I is correct: **The**Second Round Table Conference**from September to December 1931.**At this time,**Lord **Willingdon had assumed office as the Viceroy of India (1931–1936),**succeeding Lord Irwin earlier in 1931.**

Statement II is correct: Mahatma Gandhi participated in the Conference as the sole representative of the Indian **National Congress.**His participation followed the**Gandhi–Irwin Pact (1931),**under which the Congress agreed to attend the Conference while**temporarily suspending the Civil Disobedience Movement**.**

Statement III is incorrect:**The**Civil Disobedience Movement was suspended before the Conference,**as a consequence of the**Gandhi–Irwin Pact**, not because of the Conference’s success. In fact, the**Second Round Table Conference failed to reach a consensus**on key constitutional and political issues.**After Gandhi’s return to India**and the breakdown of negotiations,**the Civil Disobedience Movement was resumed, **not suspended.**

questions.questionText: What was the Dutt-Bradley Thesis? realising the independence of India. front. foremost part in realising the anti-imperialist people’s front.

options.a: The Working Committee of the Indian National Congress decided that Congress should play a crucial role in
options.b: The Socialist Party decided to play the foremost part in the anti-imperialist struggle.
options.c: Revolutionary socialist Batukeshwar Dutt put forth a ten-point plan to work for the success of the anti-imperialist
options.d: It was a Communist Party document, according to which the National Congress could play a great part and a

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **The Dutt-Bradley Thesis was a document written by Rajni Palme Dutt and Ben Bradley, two leaders of the **Communist Party. **The thesis argued that the Indian National Congress could play a great part in realizing the anti-imperialist people’s front.**

Knowledge Base: **The**Dutt-Bradley Thesis was published in International Press Correspondence (INPRECOR), the journal of **Communist International, on February 29, 1936**. It was also called the thesis for the Anti-imperialist People’s Front in India. The Dutt-Bradley Theses primarily tried to answer the following crucial questions:
- How to transform the (then existing) situation?
- How can we unite and mobilise a powerful movement of resistance to British imperialism and for the needs of the masses?
- What shall be the policy of the national representatives who are elected?
- What shall be the future line of direction of the national struggles to defeat imperialism?

statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement I: Pravasi Bharatiya Diwas is celebrated by the Government of India once every two years on 9 January.
Statement II: Mahatma Gandhi returned from South Africa to India on Jan 9, 1915.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement I: Pravasi Bharatiya Diwas is celebrated by the Government of India once every two years on 9 January. Statement II: Mahatma Gandhi returned from South Africa to India on Jan 9, 1915. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The tradition of celebrating Pravasi Bharatiya Divas (PBD) began in 2003, with the first PBD Convention held on 9 January 2003 to recognise and commemorate the contributions of the overseas Indian community to India’s development. Since 2015, under a revised format, PBD has been organised once every 2 years.**

Statement I is correct:**Pravasi Bharatiya Divas is celebrated once in every two years on 9th**January**to strengthen the engagement of the overseas Indian community with the Government of India and reconnect them with their cultural roots.
**Statement II is correct:** After completing his law studies in England, Mahatma Gandhi went to South Africa in 1893 in connection with a legal case involving his client, Dada Abdulla. In South Africa, he witnessed the ugly face of white racism and the humiliation to which Asians, who had gone there as labourers, were subjected. Gandhi decided to stay in South Africa and organise the Indian workers so that they can fight for their rights. He stayed there till 1914 after which **he returned to India on 9 January 1915.**. 9 January is observed as Pravasi Bharatiya Divas to commemorate the return of Mahatma Gandhi from South Africa to India in 1915.Hence option a is correct:**Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I**

questions.questionText: In the context of the Indian freedom struggle, who among the following was elected as the President of the first All India Khilafat Conference held at Delhi in 1919?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **The Khilafat Movement in India emerged from the deep sentiments of Indian Muslims to protect the institution of the Khalifa (Caliph) in Turkey after the First World War. On 20 March 1919, a Khilafat Committee was formed in Bombay. The**All India Khilafat Conference was held in Delhi in November 1919 and Mahatma Gandhi was elected as the **President of the first All India Khilafat Conference.** The conference passed important resolutions advocating the boycott of peace celebrations, non-cooperation with the government, and the boycott of British goods.

statementLines: Consider the following:
I. Appointment of a Congress working committee (CWC) of 15 members.
II. Abolition of any entry fee for joining the Indian National Congress.
III. Reorganisation of Provincial Congress Committees (PCCs) on a linguistic basis.
IV. Opening up of Congress membership to all men and women of the age of 21 or above.
Which of the above reforms were adopted at the Nagpur Session of the Indian National Congress (1920)?
questions.questionText: Consider the following: I. Appointment of a Congress working committee (CWC) of 15 members. II. Abolition of any entry fee for joining the Indian National Congress. III. Reorganisation of Provincial Congress Committees (PCCs) on a linguistic basis. IV. Opening up of Congress membership to all men and women of the age of 21 or above. Which of the above reforms were adopted at the Nagpur Session of the Indian National Congress (1920)?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **The annual session of the Indian National Congress (INC) was held in December 1920 in the Nagpur district of Maharashtra. It was presided over by C. Vijayaraghavachariar. During this session, the INC passed the main resolution on Non-Cooperation and many organizational changes were also made to the Congress at this session in a way to enable its functioning as a continuous political organization for the implementation of its resolutions.** Option I is correct: **The appointment of the fifteen-member Congress Working Committee, established to guide the Congress, was significant outcome of the Nagpur session.** Option II is incorrect: **At the session,** the entry fee for Congress membership was reduced to four annas(not **abolished completely). ** Option III is correct: As part of the organisational reforms adopted at the session, Provincial Congress Committees **were reorganised on a linguistic basis, **and ward-level committees were also established.** Option IV is correct:**Under the organisational reforms of the Congress in this session,**membership was opened to all **men and women above the age of 21**, and this minimum age requirement was further reduced to 18 years in 1921.

statementLines: Before launching a full scale civil disobedience campaign as one of the stages of the Non Cooperation movement,
Mahatma Gandhi suspended the movement in
1922. What is/are the possible reasons behind the suspension of the movement?
I. The resolution for full scale civil disobedience campaign was rejected by the Indian National Congress as at the time
Congress’s goal was to secure Swaraj (self-government) through constitutional means.
II. Violence in Bombay following the Prince of Wales’ visit to India convinced Gandhiji that masses have not understood the message of Non-Violence.
III. Violence in Chauri Chaura, where a number of people were injured and killed.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Before launching a full scale civil disobedience campaign as one of the stages of the Non Cooperation movement, Mahatma Gandhi suspended the movement in 1922. What is/are the possible reasons behind the suspension of the movement? I. The resolution for full scale civil disobedience campaign was rejected by the Indian National Congress as at the time Congress’s goal was to secure Swaraj (self-government) through constitutional means. II. Violence in Bombay following the Prince of Wales’ visit to India convinced Gandhiji that masses have not understood the message of Non-Violence. III. Violence in Chauri Chaura, where a number of people were injured and killed. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Gandhiji proposed that the Non Cooperation movement should unfold in stages. It should begin with the surrender of titles that the government awarded, and a boycott of civil services, army, police, courts and legislative councils, schools, and foreign goods. Then, in case the government used repression, a full civil disobedience campaign would be launched. Gandhi had planned on starting a civil disobedience movement in February 1922, in the taluks of Bardoli and Anand in Gujarat. Peasants were to be asked to stop paying taxes and refuse all cooperation with the government. If it succeeded, Gandhi had hoped to replicate the movement elsewhere in the country. But before it could start Gandhiji suspended the movement in February 1922.**

Statement I is incorrect:**The**All-India Congress Committee did not reject Civil Disobedience in principle**. In July 1921, the Congress authorised local Congress committees to initiate Civil Disobedience if it was thought that the people were ready for it. In the Indian National Congress Session of Nagpur the Congress’s previous goal to secure Swaraj (self-government) through constitutional means was changed. Now the Congress decided to attain Swaraj through peaceful and legitimate means, thus committing itself to an extraconstitutional mass struggle.**

Statement II is correct: **The visit of**Prince of Wales visited India in November 1921 **triggered one of the**major **violent outbreaks of the movement**, with attacks on Europeans, Anglo-Indians, and Parsis. It happened on the streets of Bombay. These incidents convinced**Gandhi that the masses were not yet ready to adhere to strict non-violence. **It is considered as one of the reasons behind why he did not continue with the movement. **

Statement III is correct: In Chauri-Chaura incident on February 5, 1922, **where Twenty-two policemen were killed in the violence prompted Gandhiji to withdraw the Noncooperation movement**. Gandhi felt that people had not learnt or fully understood the method of non-violence. This was the **immediate reason behind the suspension of the movement. **

questions.questionText: With reference to the History of Modern India, which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the Poona Pact, 1932? I. It was signed between the Indian National Congress and the Viceroy on behalf of the British Prime Minister. II. It provided for reservation of seats for the Depressed classes in central as well as provincial legislatures. III. Under the Pact, the Congress agreed to participate in the Second Round Table Conference. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **On September 20, 1932, Mahatma Gandhi began a fast unto death in Pune’s Yerawada Jail in protest against the award of separate electorates for Scheduled Castes.**

Statement I is incorrect. **On 24th September, 1932**B.R. Ambedkar signed the Poona Pact with Madan Mohan **Malviya, who represented Mahatma Gandhi.**The pact replaced the separate electorate with reservations for the Depressed Classes. 

Statement II is correct. **The Poona Pact contained reservation of seats for the Depressed Classes within the frame of joint electorate.**148 seats were reserved for the Depressed Classes in the provincial legislatures. In the Central **legislature, 18 per cent of the seats shall be reserved**for the Depressed Classes. Election to these seats shall be by joint electorates. The Poona Pact discarded the idea of separate electorates for the depressed classes.**

Statement III is incorrect. **The Poona Pact was signed on 24th September 1932. The second Round Table Conference was held in London from 7 September 1931 to 1 December 1931.

statementLines: With reference to the British colonial rule in India, consider the following statements:
1. Mahatma Gandhi was instrumental in the abolition of the system of ‘indentured labour’.
2. In Lord Chelmsford’s ‘War Conference’, Mahatma Gandhi did not support the resolution on recruiting Indians for
World War.
3. Consequent upon the breaking of Salt Law by Indian people, the Indian National Congress was declared illegal by the colonial rulers.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the British colonial rule in India, consider the following statements: 1. Mahatma Gandhi was instrumental in the abolition of the system of ‘indentured labour’. 2. In Lord Chelmsford’s ‘War Conference’, Mahatma Gandhi did not support the resolution on recruiting Indians for World War. 3. Consequent upon the breaking of Salt Law by Indian people, the Indian National Congress was declared illegal by the colonial rulers. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**

Statement 1 is correct.**Indian immigration to South Africa had begun in 1890 when the White settlers recruited indentured Indian labour, mainly from South India, to work on the sugar plantations. They faced racial discrimination in South Africa**During the early 1900s, Gandhi was instrumental in getting the indentured labour system abolished in the British **Empire**through the use of the method of passive resistance or civil disobedience, named Satyagraha.**

Statement 2 is incorrect.**The Viceroy of India Lord Chelmsford invited various Indian leaders to attend a War conference during World War I. Gandhi accepted the invitation and went to Delhi. Gandhi was not happy that leaders like Tilak or the Ali brothers had not been invited to the conference, so he felt unwilling to attend the conference. After meeting the Viceroy, who was very keen that Gandhi should support the resolution on recruiting Indians for World War, Gandhi**supported the government’s resolution on recruiting. He believed that whole-hearted co
- **operation with the government will bring India within sight of the goal of Swaraj as nothing else will.**

Statement 3 is correct.**Gandhiji began his salt march from Ahmedabad to Dandi on 12th March 1930. Gandhi and his selected followers reached Dandi beach and broke the salt law by picking up salt left on the shore by the sea. He wanted the people to break the salt law openly and to prepare themselves for non-violent resistance to police action. The British government took many other leaders under custody. In dealing with the breakers of the salt law, the police resorted to their usual brutal methods and**The Indian National Congress was declared illegal.**

statementLines: In the context of modern Indian history, which of the following is/are the recommendations of the Indian
Statutory Commission (Simon Commission) Report?
I. Continuation of Dyarchy in the Provinces.
II. Establishment of Responsible Government at the Centre alongwith Viceroy being accountable to the Central legislature.
III. Separation of Burma from British India.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: In the context of modern Indian history, which of the following is/are the recommendations of the Indian Statutory Commission (Simon Commission) Report? I. Continuation of Dyarchy in the Provinces. II. Establishment of Responsible Government at the Centre alongwith Viceroy being accountable to the Central legislature. III. Separation of Burma from British India. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **The Government of India Act, 1919, mandated the appointment of a commission after ten years to evaluate governance progress and suggest reforms. Accordingly, the British government established the Indian Statutory Commission (Simon Commission) to assess India's readiness for constitutional reforms. The Commission published its report in May 1930.**

Statement I is incorrect: It recommended scrapping of Dyarchy in the provinces and establishment of responsible **unitary government in provinces. **

Statement II is incorrect: The Report declared that the establishment of responsible government at the centre was **to wait indefinitely **i.e., it was to be established somewhere in the future. It recommended that a Greater India consisting of British India and the Princely States as a federal association was to be established in the future but the clause of British Paramountcy (with Viceroy as the agent of Paramount power) was to remain.**

Statement III is correct:**The** Simon Commission**report**suggested for the separation of Sindh from Bombay **province. Besides, it also recommended separation of Burma (Myanmar) from British India as it was not a natural **part of India. **

statementLines: With reference to British Colonial rule in India, the Inland Emigration Act of 1859 was primarily enacted with
which one of the following objectives?
questions.questionText: With reference to British Colonial rule in India, the Inland Emigration Act of 1859 was primarily enacted with which one of the following objectives?

options.a: To prevent the plantation workers from leaving tea gardens without permission.
options.b: To abolish the exploitative system of indentured labour.
options.c: To facilitate the supply of Indian indentured labour to other British colonies.
options.d: To prevent illegal migration of Indians to other British colonies.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The Inland Emigration Act of 1859 prohibited the** plantation workers in India from leaving the tea gardens without **permission**. The Act stipulated that the plantation workers could leave the tea gardens only after the permission of the plantation owners. The Act effectively bound the workers to the tea gardens. It severely restricted the freedom of movement of plantation workers in India. The Act was primarily aimed at securing a steady supply of labor for the rapidly expanding tea industry in Assam and other regions of the country. The Act secured the plantation owners a steady supply of labour by preventing the workers from leaving the plantation sites.

statementLines: During the freedom struggle of India, a formal resolution was passed in a session of Indian National Congress

(INC) adopting Pingali Vekaiah's/Venkayya’s designed flag as India’s flag with a little modification. While the white and green remained, the red was replaced by saffron. In this context, in which of the following sessions of INC was such a resolution passed?
questions.questionText: During the freedom struggle of India, a formal resolution was passed in a session of Indian National Congress (INC) adopting Pingali Vekaiah's/Venkayya’s designed flag as India’s flag with a little modification. While the white and green remained, the red was replaced by saffron. In this context, in which of the following sessions of INC was such a resolution passed?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The idea of a national flag for India evolved gradually during the freedom struggle. - In 1921, at the Bezwada (now Vijayawada) session of Congress, a young freedom fighter Pingali Venkaiah/Venkayya, presented a design of a flag to Mahatma Gandhi. The flag consisted of three stripes representing multiple communities living in harmony in India. A spinning wheel was superimposed in the center, signifying the country's progress. - In 1931, a formal resolution was passed adopting Pingali Vekaiah's/Venkayya’s flag with a little modification. While the white and green remained, the red was replaced by saffron. The saffron was for courage, white for peace and green for fertility and growth. Finally, in July 1947, the Constituent Assembly formally adopted the flag of free India. The spinning wheel was replaced by the Dharma Chakra of Emperor Ashoka, symbolizing truth and life. This came to be called the Tiranga. Knowledge Base:**Calcutta Session, 1928**-**President:**Motilal Nehru **-**Key significance:**o Endorsed the**Nehru Report (1928)**, which demanded**Dominion Status**for India. o Issued an ultimatum to the British Government: if Dominion Status was not granted within one year, Congress would demand**Complete Independence**.**Lahore Session, 1929 **-**President: Jawaharlal Nehru **-**Key significance:**o Adoption of the historic resolution of**Purna Swaraj (Complete Independence)**. o**26 January 1930**was declared**Independence Day**, to be celebrated annually. o Provided ideological and political groundwork for the**Civil Disobedience Movement (1930)**. o The tricolour flag was hoisted on the banks of the Ravi.**Haripura Session, 1938 **-**President:**Subhas Chandra Bose **-**Key significance:**o Strong emphasis on**planned economic development**and**state-led industrialisation**. o Proposed the setting up of a**National Planning Committee** (later chaired by Jawaharlal Nehru). o This reflected Bose’s socialist and left-leaning vision, leading to ideological differences with Gandhian leadership.

statementLines: Who among the following founded the Bhil Seva Mandal in 1922?
questions.questionText: Who among the following founded the Bhil Seva Mandal in 1922?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Amritlal Vithaldas Thakkar**, popularly known as Thakkar Bapa was an Indian social worker who worked for upliftment of tribal people in Gujarat state in India. He became a member of the Servants of India Society founded by Gopal Krishna Gokhale in 1905. In 1922, he**founded the Bhil Seva Mandal**. Later, he became the general secretary of the Harijan Sevak Sangh founded by Mahatma Gandhi in 1932.**

Knowledge Base: **-**Dayaram Gidumal Shahani **(30 June 1857 – 7 December 1927) or**Rishi Dayaram**, was a social reformer, judge, poet, and scholar in British India. He helped establish the D.J. Sindh College in Karachi and supported education for women. He was a scholar of Arabic, Persian, and Sanskrit. He founded the Seva Sadan along with Behramji Malabari. He published numerous books including Sata saheliyun and Chabak mana lai ain rihan (Lashes for the mind, 1923–29). -**Gurusaday Dutt **(1882–1941) was a civil servant, folklorist, and writer. He was the founder of the Bratachari Movement in the 1930s. In October 1925, he started Bangalakshmi, a monthly magazine that is still published. In 1929, he started a magazine called Gramer Daak that dealt with agrarian and rural matters of concern. -**Dhondo Keshav Karve **(18 April 1858 – 9 November 1962), popularly known as Maharshi Karve, was a social reformer in India in the field of women’s welfare. He founded the first women’s university in India-SNDT
Women’s University.

statementLines: With reference to the Mahatma Gandhi’s works/activities in South Africa, consider the following statements:
I. He set up the Natal Indian Congress to safeguard the political rights of Indians in Natal.
II. He started the newspaper Indian Opinion to mobilize public opinion against racial discrimination.
III. He established the Phoenix Settlement and Tolstoy Farm as centers for community living and satyagraha training.
IV. He founded the All-India Spinners’ Association during his stay in South Africa to promote khadi among the Indian diaspora.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Mahatma Gandhi’s works/activities in South Africa, consider the following statements: I. He set up the Natal Indian Congress to safeguard the political rights of Indians in Natal. II. He started the newspaper Indian Opinion to mobilize public opinion against racial discrimination. III. He established the Phoenix Settlement and Tolstoy Farm as centers for community living and satyagraha training. IV. He founded the All-India Spinners’ Association during his stay in South Africa to promote khadi among the Indian diaspora. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Mahatma Gandhi stayed in South Africa from 1893 to 1914**, a period that proved to be a turning point in his life and political philosophy. During these years, he confronted racial discrimination and legal injustice, which led him to evolve methods of**non-violent resistance (satyagraha)**. His South African experience shaped his ideas on truth, civil rights, mass mobilization, and moral politics, laying the foundation for his later leadership of the Indian national movement.**

Statement I is correct: Mahatma Gandhi established the Natal Indian Congress**(NIC)**in 1894 in South Africa**to organize Indians politically and to**protect their civil and political rights against discriminatory laws and racial **discrimination**in Natal. This was Mahatma Gandhi’s first major political organization.**

Statement II is correct: Mahatma Gandhi started the weekly newspaper Indian Opinion in 1903 in South Africa,**to create awareness among Indians and the wider public about racial discrimination, injustice, and the principles of truth and non-violence. It became an important instrument for mobilizing public opinion.**

Statement III is correct: Mahatma Gandhi founded the Phoenix Settlement in 1904 near Durban (Natal),**inspired by his reading of**John Ruskin’s Unto This Last**, which deeply influenced his ideas on equality, dignity of labour, and critique of capitalism. - Later,**in 1910, he established Tolstoy Farm near Johannesburg**(Transvaal) on land donated by his close associate**Hermann Kallenbach**. The farm was named after the Russian writer and moral thinker Leo Tolstoy, whom Gandhi greatly admired. - Both settlements functioned as experiments in community living, self-reliance, moral discipline, and collective labour, and served as practical training centres for satyagraha, preparing volunteers for disciplined and non-violent resistance.**

Statement IV is incorrect: **The**All-India Spinners’ Association was founded by Mahatma Gandhi in 1925 in India, **long after his return from South Africa. ** Its purpose was to promote khadi, rural employment, and economic self-sufficiency as part of the constructive programme of the national movement.

statementLines: In the context of the Indian national movement, who came to be known as the “No-Changers” within the
Congress after the withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement?
The Leaders of the Congress:
questions.questionText: In the context of the Indian national movement, who came to be known as the “No-Changers” within the Congress after the withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement? The Leaders of the Congress:

options.a: Who opposed council entry and insisted on continuing with the grassroot constructive programmes.
options.b: Who demanded an end to the policy of non-violence and advocated revolutionary activities.
options.c: Who believed in the cooperation with the British administration to secure constitutional reforms.
options.d: Who believed that mass civil disobedience should be resumed immediately without any preparatory work.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **After the withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement in 1922**following the Chauri Chaura incident, the Indian National Congress faced a serious internal debate regarding the future strategy of the national movement. This**led to **the emergence of two distinct groups, each advocating a different course of action:**1**. No-Changers: **The No-Changers**opposed entry into the legislative councils**and argued that council participation would legitimize colonial rule and dilute the spirit of non-cooperation. Instead, they emphasized** constructive **programmes as the primary method of struggle**, which included: ○ Promotion of khadi and village industries ○ Development of national education outside government control ○ Social reforms such as the removal of untouchability and upliftment of villages They also advocated for**continuation of boycott of government offices**and preparation for the future resumption of civil disobedience. Leading proponents of this group included**C. Rajagopalachari, Vallabhbhai Patel, Rajendra Prasad **and MA Ansari. **2.**Pro-Changers (Swarajists):**The Pro-Changers or Swarajists wanted the constructive programme to be coupled with a political programme of council-entry. The matter came to a head in December 1922 at the Gaya Session of the Congress where Rajagopalachari led opposition to Council Entry forcing C.R. Das to tender resignation from the presidentship of the Congress. On being outvoted C.R. Das announced the formation of the Swaraj Party on 31 December 1922 with himself as President and Motilal as Secretary.

statementLines: With reference to the Indian freedom struggle, consider the following incidents:
I. Assasination of British Police officer John Saunders
II. Meerut Conspiracy Case
III. Assassination of Curzon-Wyllie, a British official
IV. Assassination of Michael O’Dwyer, a British officer In
how many of the above incidents was Bhagat Singh actively involved?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian freedom struggle, consider the following incidents: I. Assasination of British Police officer John Saunders II. Meerut Conspiracy Case III. Assassination of Curzon-Wyllie, a British official IV. Assassination of Michael O’Dwyer, a British officer In how many of the above incidents was Bhagat Singh actively involved?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. Bhagat Singh occupies a**unique position in the revolutionary phase of the Indian national movement**. He was not merely a participant in violent action, but also a**political thinker and organiser**who believed in revolutionary transformation rooted in ideas.** Option I is correct**. In December** 1928, Bhagat Singh and Rajguru**shot dead a 21-year-old British police officer,** John **P Saunders**, in Lahore, in what was a case of mistaken identity. The plan was to kill senior British superintendent James Scott for his role in the death of Lala Lajpat Rai, a month earlier, during a protest against the Simon Commission.** Option II is incorrect**. During the period of**1920-29**, a series of conspiracy cases had been leveled against the Communists, including the**Meerut Conspiracy case (1929)**which resulted in harsh prison sentences. Bhagat Singh was**not an accused or participant**in this case.  Option III is incorrect**. The**assassination of Curzon-Wyllie took place in 1909 in London**. It was carried out by**Madan Lal Dhingra**, much before Bhagat Singh emerged as a revolutionary figure. Bhagat Singh had**no connection**with this incident in terms of planning or execution.** Option IV is incorrect**. The**assassination of Michael O’Dwyer occurred in 1940**, long after Bhagat Singh had been executed. It was carried out by**Sardar Udham Singh**as an act of retribution for the**Jallianwala Bagh massacre**.

statementLines: The ‘All- Bengal Students Conference’ in September 1928 was presided over by:
questions.questionText: The ‘All- Bengal Students Conference’ in September 1928 was presided over by:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. In the history of the Indian Independence Movement, students, all over the subcontinent played a significant and active role. Their participation proved to be an important aspect in all the programs and movements. Students had organized themselves under several organizations, All-Bengal Students Association being one of them. It was formed in 1928, and the office bearers were students studying in Kolkata (formerly Calcutta) district of the state of West Bengal. After several meetings, its final shape was given during a four-day conference**held in September 1928**. The conference was presided over by Jawaharlal Nehru**, and thus the All-Bengal Students Association came into existence in 1928.

statementLines: In the context of the Indian freedom struggle, Shanti Ghosh and Bina Das are best known for involvement in
which of the following activities?
questions.questionText: In the context of the Indian freedom struggle, Shanti Ghosh and Bina Das are best known for involvement in which of the following activities?

options.a: Participating in revolutionary activities like attacks on British officials in Bengal
options.b: Organising the anti-Chaukidari tax campaign in Bihar
options.c: Leading tribal uprisings against forest laws in eastern India
options.d: Mobilising mill workers during the Non-Cooperation Movement

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** option a is the correct answer**. The revolutionary phase of the Indian national movement, especially in the 1920s and 1930s, witnessed**a notable **participation of young men and women**. In Bengal, revolutionary activity acquired a distinct character marked by**group action, symbolic attacks on colonial authority, and active involvement of women. **These revolutionaries sought to**challenge British supremacy through dramatic acts of defiance**, aimed at inspiring the youth and demoralising the colonial administration.**Santi Ghosh**was a school student from Comilla who, along with Suniti Choudhury,**assassinated the British District **Magistrate**as an act of revolutionary resistance. Her action was part of a broader revolutionary current in Bengal that emphasised**direct attacks on symbols of colonial authority**.**Bina Das**became widely known for her**attempt on the life of the Governor of Bengal**in 1932, carried out during her college convocation ceremony. Her act was deliberately public and symbolic, intended to convey that**even the **highest representatives of the colonial state were not beyond challenge**.

statementLines: Consider the following subjects with regard to Non-Cooperation Programme:
I. Boycott of law-courts and foreign cloth
II. Observance of strict non-violence
III. Retention of titles and honours without using them in public
IV. Establishment of Panchayats for settling disputes
How many of the above were parts of the Non-Cooperation Programme?
questions.questionText: Consider the following subjects with regard to Non-Cooperation Programme: I. Boycott of law-courts and foreign cloth II. Observance of strict non-violence III. Retention of titles and honours without using them in public IV. Establishment of Panchayats for settling disputes How many of the above were parts of the Non-Cooperation Programme?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The Non-Cooperation Movement was the first nationwide movement led by Mahatma Gandhi in 1920, urging Indians to withdraw support from British institutions and refuse cooperation with colonial rule. In September 1920, at a special session in Calcutta, the Indian National Congress (INC) approved a non-cooperation programme till the Punjab and Khilafat wrongs were removed and swaraj was established. The programme was to include:** Option I is correct: **The**Programme of Non-Cooperation included boycotts of law courts, **government schools and colleges. It also included the**boycott of foreign cloth and use of khadi instead; **also practice of hand-spinning to be done. - As a reaction to the movement, thousands of students left schools and colleges and joined 800 national schools and colleges that had come up throughout the country. - Many leading lawyers of the country like C.R. Das, Motilal Nehru, Saifuddin Kitchlew, C. Rajagopalachari, M.R. Jayakar, Vallabhbhai Patel, Asaf Ali and T. Prakasham quit their practice. - Heaps of foreign cloth were burnt publicly and their imports fell by half.**Option II and IV are correct: **As part of the Non-Cooperation Programme, it was decided to set up national schools and colleges,**establish and strengthen the panchayats for settlement of disputes, **promotion of hand spinning and weaving, condemnation and renunciation of untouchability,**maintenance of communal amity and strict observance **of non-violence. ** Option III is incorrect: **The Non-Cooperation Programme**involved the renunciation of government titles and **honorary positions (not the retention of titles and honours without using them in public). **

statementLines: With reference to Indian freedom struggle, consider the following passage:
In 1928-29 he was elected a member of the committee to associate with the Indian Statutory Commission (Simon
Commission). He was one of the first legislators in India to introduce a Bill in the Bombay Legislative council for the abolition of serfdom of agricultural tenants.
Which of the following leaders is described in the above passage?
questions.questionText: With reference to Indian freedom struggle, consider the following passage: In 1928-29 he was elected a member of the committee to associate with the Indian Statutory Commission (Simon Commission). He was one of the first legislators in India to introduce a Bill in the Bombay Legislative council for the abolition of serfdom of agricultural tenants. Which of the following leaders is described in the above passage?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Dr. Ambedkar's parliamentary career began when he was nominated as a member of the Bombay Legislative **Council in 1926**, the membership of which he held till 1934. Later, he played a very effective and purposeful role as the Opposition Leader in the Bombay Legislative Assembly and showed his effectiveness as a seasoned parliamentarian.**On 17 September 1937, Ambedkar introduced a Bill in the Bombay Legislative Council for the abolition of the Khoti **system**in Konkan, a position more radical than his party’s programme. It must be noted that**in the first Provincial **Assemblies, Ambedkar was the first legislator in India to introduce a Bill for the abolition of serfdom of **agricultural tenants. **In this struggle, communists too were involved. The climax was a march of 25,000 peasants to the Bombay Council Hall on 12 January 1938, the biggest pre-independence mobilisation of peasants in Maharashtra.
After this protest march, Ambedkar delivered a speech and he declared his closeness to Marxist ideology.

statementLines: “He was a sanyasi who worked in Fiji as an indentured labourer. He organised peasants in Awadh and founded the Oudh Kisan Sabha in October 1920.”
Who among the following is referred to in the above passage?
questions.questionText: “He was a sanyasi who worked in Fiji as an indentured labourer. He organised peasants in Awadh and founded the Oudh Kisan Sabha in October 1920.” Who among the following is referred to in the above passage?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Option a is incorrect. **Radhey Shyam Sharma was an active participant in the freedom movement of India. He participated in the Quit India Movement (1942).**

Option b is incorrect. **Indulal Yagnik was a leader of the All India Kisan Sabha. He is mostly remembered as the force behind the Mahagujarat Movement of the 1950s.**

Option c is incorrect. **Shripad Amrit Dange was a founding member of the Communist Party of India (CPI). He was a prominent trade unionist and also the president of the Communist Party of India. Dange also served as joint secretary, and later president, of the All India Trade Union Congress.**

Option d is correct. Baba Ramchandra**was a sanyasi who had been to Fiji as an indentured labourer. He came back in 1917. Around 1920, he organised peasants in Awadh. The movement was against talukdars and landlords who demanded exorbitantly high rents and a variety of other cesses from peasants. The peasant movement demanded reduction of revenue, abolition of begar, and social boycott of oppressive landlords.**Baba Ramchandra, along with Jawaharlal Nehru and a few others, set up the Oudh Kisan Sabha**in October 1920.

questions.microTopic: Constitutional Developments
statementLines: With reference to Modern Indian history, consider the following:
I. Establishment of Federal constitution with residual powers to the Provinces
II. A joint mixed electorate throughout India for the election to the House of Representatives and the provincial legislatures.
III. The redistribution of provinces to take place on a linguistic basis.
IV. The right to form associations or unions.
How many of the above given were the recommendations of the Nehru Report which was an outline draft of a constitution at the time?
questions.questionText: With reference to Modern Indian history, consider the following: I. Establishment of Federal constitution with residual powers to the Provinces II. A joint mixed electorate throughout India for the election to the House of Representatives and the provincial legislatures. III. The redistribution of provinces to take place on a linguistic basis. IV. The right to form associations or unions. How many of the above given were the recommendations of the Nehru Report which was an outline draft of a constitution at the time?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **In response to the Simon Commission of 1927, the Indian National Congress, at its Madras Session in 1927, passed a resolution to appoint a committee for drafting a "Swaraj Constitution for India." This committee, chaired by Motilal Nehru, came to be known as the Nehru Committee, and it submitted its report in August 1928.** Option I is incorrect: The Nehru Report **proposed the establishment of a responsible government at both the Centre and in the provinces but** did not contain virtually any federal features.**In contrast Muhammad Ali Jinnah’s**Fourteen Points demanded a federal constitution with residuary powers vested **in the provinces along with real provincial autonomy. This was not included in the Nehru Report. ** Option II is correct: **The Nehru Report rejected the system of separate electorates. Instead, it demanded**there shall **he joint mixed electorates throughout India for the House of Representatives and the provincial legislatures. **It further said that there shall be no reservation of seats for the House of Representatives except for Muslims in provinces where they are in a minority and non-Muslims in the North West Frontier Province. Such reservation will be in strict proportion to the Muslim population in every province where they are in a minority and in proportion to the non-Muslim population in North West Frontier Province. The Muslims or non-Muslims where reservation is allowed to them shall have the right to contest additional seats.** Option III is correct:**T**he Nehru report recommended redistribution of provinces should take place on a linguistic **basis **on the demand of the majority of the population of the area concerned, subject to financial and administrative considerations.** Option IV is correct:**The Nehru Report emphasized the importance of securing Fundamental Rights that had been denied by the colonial government**. It recommended nineteen fundamental rights**, including the right of free expression of opinion, as well as the right to assemble peaceably and without arms, and**to form associations or **unions. **

statementLines: Who among the following leaders led the first batch of Satyagrahis to break the Salt law from Cuttack to Inchudi in Balasore sea-coast in Orissa in April 1930?
questions.questionText: Who among the following leaders led the first batch of Satyagrahis to break the Salt law from Cuttack to Inchudi in Balasore sea-coast in Orissa in April 1930?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The**Civil Disobedience Movement**(**1930-19**34 was**launched under the leadership of Mahatma Gandhi **with the aim of openly and peacefully**defying unjust colonial laws**. It was**formally launched**by Mahatma Gandhi**on 6 April 1930**, when he picked up a handful of salt at Dandi after completing the historic**Dandi March**from Sabarmati Ashram to Dandi, thereby deliberately breaking the colonial salt law.**

Option a is correct: Gopabandhu Chaudhuri**was a noted Gandhian leader in Odisha. Gopabandhu Choudhury led the first batch of Satyagrahis from Cuttack to Inchudi in Balasore sea-coast in Orissa. On 7th April, 1930, The Prajatantra reported about this. The movement was prominent in coastal areas like**Balasore, Cuttack, and nearby districts (e.g., **Puri),**where salt was manufactured illegally in defiance of British laws. Inchudi is often called the "**second Dandi**" of India.**

Option b is incorrect:**Kanaklata Barua**was an Indian freedom fighter from Assam. During the Quit India Movement she joined the Mrityu Bahini, a death squad comprising groups of youth from the Gohpur sub division of Assam.**

Option c is incorrect: Chandraprabha Saikiani incited aboriginal Kachari villages to break forest laws in December **1930. **

Option d is incorrect:**Pulin Behari Das** was an Indian revolutionary and the founder-president of Dhaka Anushilan Samiti. He founded the National School in Dhaka used as a training ground for raising revolutionary forces. He masterminded the plot to eliminate Basil Copleston Allen, the erstwhile District Magistrate of Dhaka. In 1908 he organized the sensational Barrah Dacoity.
